one_line_takeaway: LLM/LM-enhanced GNNs are measurably more robust to structural and textual poisoning than shallow-embedding baselines -- an effect the authors trace to embedding quality (separability, label/structural information retention) rather than pretraining data leakage -- but combining structural and textual attacks and evading their proposed graph-purification defense remain open, only partially addressed problems.
key_finding: LLM-enhanced GNNs are consistently and substantially more robust to poisoning than the shallow-embedding baseline. Under Nettack at high perturbation, GCN with shallow embeddings drops to only 58.15%/70.93%/75.12%/56.39% accuracy on Cora/Pubmed/Ogbn-products/Tape-arxiv23, while the best LLM-enhanced variants reach 67.56%/84.76%/85.84%/67.49% on the same datasets/attack. E5-Large is frequently the strongest or second-strongest enhancer; GraphSAGE is the most robust backbone overall. Critically, a controlled leakage experiment (varying train/test overlap 0-100% on the post-cutoff Tape-arxiv23 dataset) shows robustness gains persist even at 0% overlap, and RDA stays roughly stable across overlap rates -- meaning the robustness advantage is not primarily an artifact of ground-truth leakage into LLM pretraining, but stems from the architectural integration of LLMs/LMs producing embeddings with higher separability (lower Davies-Bouldin Index, higher Silhouette Score) and better preservation of label/structural information (higher homophily, mutual information, neighbor consistency). The authors' novel combined structural+textual attack is generally more effective than either attack type alone (sentence-level textual + structural being the strongest combination in most cases), though TAPE embeddings sometimes paradoxically benefit from added textual perturbation. Their proposed graph purification defense (filtering edges via LLM/LM embedding cosine similarity) substantially raises accuracy under attack on Pubmed and Tape-arxiv23, in several cases exceeding clean-graph accuracy, and generalizes better than the RGCN/SimpGCN baselines, which were inconsistent across datasets (e.g., SimpGCN's defense caused sizeable accuracy drops of 28-31% on Cora).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a systematic robustness-assessment framework for LLM-enhanced Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) under poisoning attacks. It empirically evaluates 24 victim models (8 LLM/LM-based feature enhancers x 3 GNN backbones: GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) against 6 structural poisoning attacks (3 targeted: Nettack, SGA, NAG-R; 3 non-targeted: Mettack, DICE, PGA) and 3 textual poisoning attacks at character/word/sentence granularity (DeepWordBug, BERT-Attack, MAYA) on four real-world text-attributed graph (TAG) datasets, one of which (Tape-arxiv23) postdates common LLM pretraining cutoffs to control for ground-truth leakage. Beyond the benchmark itself, the authors also contribute (a) an in-depth causal analysis of why LLM-enhanced GNNs are more robust (embedding separability, homophily, mutual information, neighbor consistency), (b) a novel combined structural+textual attack, and (c) a graph purification defense that filters edges using cosine similarity between LLM/LM-generated node embeddings.
threat_model: Gray-box poisoning: the adversary has full access to the graph structure, node textual attributes, and labels of a subset of nodes, but no knowledge of the victim model's architecture or parameters (the paper explicitly notes this is more practical than white-box and reflects public-data scenarios like citation networks on Google Scholar or e-commerce co-purchase graphs). Attack capability spans two modalities: (1) structural poisoning via edge insertion/deletion using established gray-box attacks (Nettack, SGA, NAG-R for targeted misclassification of specific nodes; Mettack, DICE, PGA for non-targeted overall performance degradation), and (2) textual poisoning via evasion-style perturbations injected into node text attributes at the character (DeepWordBug), word (BERT-Attack), and sentence (MAYA paraphrasing) levels -- explicitly not direct poisoning of the LLM/LM itself, which the authors deem too costly. The stated attacker goal is either targeted misclassification (e.g., causing a fraudulent e-commerce node to be classified as legitimate to evade fraud detection) or general accuracy degradation.
limitations: The authors' own stated scope limits: they restrict evaluation to 3 classic GNN backbones (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) and 8 feature enhancers, explicitly excluding more advanced architectures (GIN, GAT-v2) and models (GPT-4) 'to ensure reproducibility and fair comparison with established baselines' -- meaning generalization to newer/larger models is untested. All poisoning attacks are conducted in a gray-box, full-structural/textual-knowledge setting; the authors themselves flag in Section 6 that this does not hold in privacy-sensitive domains where an adversary only has partial structural knowledge, and note this as unexplored future work. The paper acknowledges textual attacks are 'relatively ineffective' compared to structural ones and often mitigated by the GNN itself. The graph purification defense's effectiveness is explicitly stated to depend on threshold selection correlated with dataset homophily, i.e., it is not a parameter-free, universally tuned method. Large datasets (Ogbn-products, Tape-arxiv23) are node-sampled subsets rather than full graphs, and target-node sampling rates differ per dataset (10% for Pubmed/Ogbn-products, 50% for Tape-arxiv23) for targeted attacks, which could affect cross-dataset comparability.

datasets_used: Cora (2,708 nodes, 5,429 edges, 7 classes, BoW features, citation network)
Pubmed (19,717 nodes, 44,338 edges, 3 classes, TF-IDF features, citation network)
Ogbn-products subset (12,011 nodes, 21,987 edges, 47 classes, BoW features, Amazon co-purchase e-commerce network)
Tape-arxiv23 subset (13,167 nodes, 23,735 edges, 40 classes, Word2Vec features, CS arXiv citation network published 2023+, chosen as post-LLM-cutoff data)
quantitative_results: Under Nettack (targeted structural attack, high perturbation), GCN+shallow embeddings accuracy: 58.15% (Cora), 70.93% (Pubmed), 75.12% (Ogbn-products), 56.39% (Tape-arxiv23)
Under the same attack, best LLM-enhanced GCN accuracy: 67.56% (Cora), 84.76% (Pubmed), 85.84% (Ogbn-products), 67.49% (Tape-arxiv23)
Clean-graph GCN accuracy gains over shallow baseline: up to 4.89% (Cora), 1.91% (Pubmed), 4.85% (Ogbn-products), 13.55% (Tape-arxiv23)

Leakage-control experiment: E5-Large on Tape-arxiv23 at 0% train/test overlap still shows the LLM-enhanced robustness advantage over baseline; RDA remains largely stable across overlap rates 0-100%, indicating gains are not primarily leakage-driven

Graph purification defense on GCN/Mettack: TAPE embeddings improve from 84.91% (clean) / 84.26% (attacked, no defense) to 86.26% (with defense) on Ogbn-products; on Pubmed, TAPE reaches 94.23% with defense vs. 84.91% attacked (exceeding the 86.21% clean baseline)
Baseline defense SimpGCN degrades accuracy by 28-32% on Cora across Mettack/DICE/PGA attacks, illustrating inconsistent generalizability of prior defenses

Combined structural+textual attack (Mettack + sentence-level MAYA) reduces Ogbn-products E5-Large accuracy from 83.23% (structural-only) to as low as 77.08% (with MAYA added, -7.63%), the largest degradation among tested combinations
Embedding separability on Pubmed: Davies-Bouldin Index drops from 8.29 (shallow embeddings) to 0.57 (TAPE); Silhouette Score rises from 1.37 to 64.29
baselines_compared: Shallow embedding baseline (BoW/TF-IDF/Word2Vec, dataset-dependent) as the primary non-LLM comparator across all 24 victim models
8 LLM/LM feature enhancers: TAPE, KEA (LLM-Explanation); LLaMA-2-7b, OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (TE3L), Linq-Embed-Mistral (LLM-Embedding); SimTeG, E5-Large, ModernBERT-large (LM-Embedding)
Structural attacks: Nettack, SGA, NAG-R (targeted); Mettack, DICE, PGA (non-targeted)
Defense baselines for the proposed graph purification method: RGCN (model-level) and SimpGCN (input-level)
